topic.title: HTML SVG Graphics
topic.description: SVG embeds resolution-independent vector graphics directly in HTML with full CSS and DOM access.

content.text: SVG in HTML: Inline, External, and Accessible

text.value: ) is an XML-based format for two-dimensional vector graphics. SVG images scale to any size without pixelation, making them ideal for logos, icons, illustrations, and data visualisations. SVG can be embedded in HTML in three ways:
(the SVG markup sits directly in the HTML document),
(treated as a raster image, no DOM access), or
(decorative, no accessibility). Inline SVG gives full access to individual elements via CSS and JavaScript, supports
for accessibility, and eliminates an extra network request. The root
attribute to scale correctly at any size. Reference:

content.title: SVG viewBox Is Required for Scalability
content.text: The viewBox attribute defines the coordinate system of the SVG canvas. Without it, the SVG renders at a fixed pixel size and cannot scale responsively. The format is viewBox='minX minY width height'. For a standard icon: viewBox='0 0 24 24'. Setting width and height to 100% in CSS scales the SVG to any container size while viewBox maintains proportions.

examples.title: Inline SVG Shapes, Paths, and CSS Styling
examples.description: This example shows inline SVG with basic shape elements, a path element with d attribute, SVG text, CSS animations on SVG elements, and the viewBox coordinate system. It demonstrates that SVG elements respond to CSS hover, transitions, and custom properties like any HTML element.
examples.code_snippet:

1. Basic Shapes

2. Path Element

3. CSS Hover on SVG Shapes (hover each card)

4. SVG Text

5. CSS Animation on SVG
examples.output: Page renders five SVG sections. The basic shapes section shows a blue rounded rectangle, purple circle, teal ellipse, green diagonal line, and red triangle. The path section shows a diamond built with L commands, a purple bezier arc with a drawing animation, and a green checkmark inside a circle that draws itself on load. The hover section shows four blue cards that lift and turn purple individually on hover. The text section shows 'DevDocs India' in a blue-to-purple gradient. The animation section shows a pulsing concentric circle with a white checkmark.

examples.title: Accessible SVG Icons and an SVG Sprite System
examples.description: This example builds accessible inline SVG icons using title and desc elements for screen readers, demonstrates the SVG sprite pattern using symbol and use elements for reusable icon libraries, and shows the img src embedding method with alt text as the simplest accessible approach for decorative contexts.

Avoid inline SVG for very complex illustrations with hundreds of paths; the HTML payload grows large and parsing slows down the initial render.

SVG images scale to any resolution without pixelation, unlike raster formats, making them ideal for HiDPI screens and responsive layouts.
Inline SVG eliminates the extra network request required by
SVG files are typically 60 to 80 percent smaller than equivalent PNG images for geometric shapes and icons, reducing total page weight significantly.

subtopics.title: SVG Coordinate System and viewBox
subtopics.description: The SVG coordinate system and viewBox attribute map internal units to CSS display size for scaling.

text.value: Every SVG element lives inside a
, defines the extent of this coordinate system. The browser maps this internal space to the CSS size of the
attribute controls how the viewBox is fitted when the CSS aspect ratio differs from the viewBox ratio. Nested

content.title: Remove width and height, Keep viewBox
content.text: For inline SVG icons meant to be sized by CSS, remove the width and height SVG attributes and keep only viewBox. Then set width and height in CSS. The viewBox establishes the proportions; CSS controls the display size. Adding explicit SVG width and height attributes prevents CSS from overriding them in some browsers.

examples.title: viewBox, preserveAspectRatio, and Coordinate System Transforms
examples.description: This example demonstrates the viewBox coordinate system by showing the same shapes at different viewBox values, the effect of preserveAspectRatio on non-square viewBoxes, and how transform translate and scale create sub-coordinate systems inside a group element.
examples.code_snippet:
SVG Coordinate System and viewBox

1. How viewBox maps internal units to display size

viewBox="0 0 100 100" — circle at cx=50

viewBox="0 0 200 200" — same cx=50, different position

2. preserveAspectRatio

preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMid meet"

preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMid slice"

3. transform: translate, scale, rotate
examples.output: Page renders three sections. The first shows two 200x200 SVG containers with identical circles at cx=50 cy=50: in the 100x100 viewBox the circle is centred, in the 200x200 viewBox the same cx=50 places it in the top-left quadrant, illustrating how viewBox changes the meaning of coordinates. The second shows two versions of a wide viewBox in a square container: meet letterboxes the shape with whitespace, slice fills and crops. The third shows four shapes in a row: the base grey rectangle, a blue version shifted with translate, a purple version scaled to 60%, and a teal version rotated 20 degrees.

text.value: is a camera pointed at the SVG canvas. The four numbers define what part of the infinite SVG coordinate plane the camera frames.
frames a 24-unit square starting at the origin. A
frames the same area but centres the origin, useful for symmetric shapes drawn around zero.
group creates a new coordinate system for everything inside it.
shifts the origin 120 units to the right.
makes every unit inside the group half as large.
spins the axis. These transforms stack: a group with both translate and scale applies them in the order listed.
For icons that need a transparent hit area larger than the visible graphic, add an invisible
. Without it, a 2px stroke icon may have a tiny click area; with it, the entire viewBox bounds are interactive even though the invisible rect is not visible.

subtopics.title: SVG Shapes and Path Elements
subtopics.description: SVG provides rect, circle, ellipse, line, polyline, polygon, and path for drawing vector shapes.

text.value: SVG has seven basic shape elements:

as attributes or CSS properties. Reference:

rows: x, y, width, height, rx, ry
Dots, avatars, progress rings, pie charts
Axis lines, dividers, chart grid lines
Any shape: icons, logos, curves, arcs

examples.title: SVG Shapes Reference and a Path-Built Icon Set
examples.description: This example shows all seven shape elements with their key attributes annotated, then builds three common UI icons entirely from path elements to demonstrate the d attribute command vocabulary. It also shows stroke-dasharray and stroke-dashoffset for the drawing animation technique.

examples.output: Page renders three sections. The first shows all seven shape elements side by side with monospace labels: blue rounded rect, purple circle, teal ellipse, green diagonal line, red polyline chart, yellow triangle polygon, and a green path checkmark. The second shows five path command demonstrations: a filled triangle using M L Z, a dashed box using H V, a cubic bezier curve animating in, a quadratic bezier animating in, and an arc animating in. The third shows four UI icons built from path elements with labels: home, bell, settings gear, and search.

subtopics.title: SVG Accessibility
subtopics.description: Accessible SVG uses role, title, desc, and aria attributes so screen readers convey image meaning.

text.value: without accessibility attributes is announced inconsistently across screen readers. The three patterns cover all cases.

rows: Decorative icon in button with text

title + desc + long description or data table

examples.title: All SVG Accessibility Patterns in One Reference
examples.description: This example implements all five SVG accessibility contexts: decorative icon, icon-only button, standalone meaningful SVG, complex chart with a data table fallback, and interactive SVG element. Each is annotated with the ARIA attributes required and the screen reader announcement it produces.
examples.code_snippet:
SVG Accessibility Patterns

1. Decorative Icon (aria-hidden)

2. Icon-Only Button (aria-label on button)

3. Standalone Meaningful SVG (role=img + title)

4. Complex Chart with Data Table Alternative

5. Interactive SVG Element (role + tabindex)
examples.output: Page renders five SVG accessibility patterns. The decorative icon button shows a save icon beside 'Save changes' text; screen readers skip the SVG and announce only the button label. The icon-only close button uses an aria-label. The standalone chart SVG shows a line chart with title and desc elements for screen reader announcement. The complex chart shows a bar chart with aria-hidden while a real accessible data table below provides the numbers. The interactive SVG element shows a blue button built from a g element with role='button' and tabindex='0'.

text.value: The accessibility contract for SVG mirrors that for
: every graphic must either have a text alternative or be hidden from assistive technology.
on an img: it tells assistive tools this element carries no information worth announcing.
Complex data charts present a harder accessibility problem than icons. A line chart with 50 data points cannot have a useful 50-word alt text. The recommended pattern is to hide the SVG with
and provide the same data in an accessible
nearby. Sighted users see the visual chart. Screen reader users navigate the table. Both get the same information through the medium that works best for them.
through the page with VoiceOver to verify that decorative SVGs are not in the tab order and that meaningful SVGs are announced correctly. Also set
on every decorative inline SVG as some versions of IE and legacy Edge make SVG elements focusable by default.

subtopics.title: SVG Sprite Pattern
subtopics.description: The SVG sprite pattern defines icons once in symbol elements and reuses them via use references.

text.value: The SVG sprite pattern stores all icons in a single hidden
element is never rendered directly; it is only a definition.

examples.title: Inline SVG Sprite Sheet with currentColor Theming
examples.description: This example builds a complete inline SVG sprite sheet, demonstrates that the same symbol can be rendered at different sizes and colours with a single use element each, shows how currentColor inherits from CSS, and demonstrates the external sprite file pattern using href pointing to an SVG file.
examples.code_snippet:
SVG Sprite Pattern

1. Same Symbol at Multiple Sizes

2. Same Symbol, Different Colors via currentColor

3. Navigation Bar Using Sprite Icons

Home Search Alerts

4. Button with Sprite Icon

5. External Sprite File Pattern

<!-- External sprite: href points to a static SVG file -->
<svg class="icon-md" aria-hidden="true" focusable="false">
  <use href="/icons/sprite.svg#sp-home"></use>
</svg>

<!-- Note: external href only works same-origin or with CORS headers.
     Inline sprite avoids the CORS restriction entirely. -->
examples.output: Page renders the hidden sprite sheet block followed by four demo sections. The sizes section shows the same bell icon at 16px, 24px, 48px, and 64px, all from a single symbol definition. The colour section shows five identical star icons in blue, purple, green, red, and gold, all using CSS color with currentColor. The navigation bar shows a dark nav row with home, search, and alerts icons beside link text. The button shows a white checkmark icon beside 'Mark complete' text. The external sprite code block shows the href pattern for file-based sprites.
